questions.questionText: Nephrotic syndrome in children is most commonly due to:
questions.optionB: Minimal change disease (MCD — 80% of childhood nephrotic syndrome, excellent response to steroids)
questions.explanation: MCD: most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (80%, peak 2-6 years). Nephrotic syndrome: (1) Heavy proteinuria (>40 mg/m²/hr or >3.5 g/day), (2) Hypoalbuminemia (<2.5 g/dL), (3) Edema (periorbital → generalized/anasarca), (4) Hyperlipidemia. MCD: light microscopy — normal. Electron microscopy — podocyte foot process effacement (hallmark). Steroid-responsive (>90% respond to prednisolone). Complications: infections (pneumococcal peritonitis — loss of IgG/complement), thromboembolism (loss of antithrombin III), hyperlipidemia. Reference: OP Ghai — Nephrotic Syndrome.

questions.questionText: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) in children presents with:
questions.optionB: Nephritic syndrome (hematuria, oliguria, edema, hypertension) 1-3 weeks after Group A Strep pharyngitis or 3-6 weeks after skin infection
questions.explanation: PSGN: most common glomerulonephritis in children. Immune complex-mediated (NOT direct infection). Occurs: 1-3 weeks after GAS pharyngitis, or 3-6 weeks after impetigo (skin). Nephritic syndrome: hematuria (coca-cola/smoky urine), edema (periorbital), hypertension, oliguria, elevated creatinine. Labs: low C3 (returns to normal in 6-8 weeks), positive ASO titer (pharyngitis)/anti-DNase B (skin). Biopsy (rarely needed): diffuse proliferative GN, subepithelial humps (EM), starry-sky pattern (IF). Self-limited (>95% recover fully in children). Reference: OP Ghai — PSGN.
